description: REVIEW REQUIRED. LLM-extracted claims and LLM-PROPOSED relationships. Inspect and approve the relationships before running the engine. Reliability is from document metadata, not LLM-judged.

claims.claim_text: The great landmasses of the Earth are not fixed in place.
claims.source: Drifting-continents monograph (1915), dismissed by the geological establishment of the day

claims.claim_text: The landmasses have drifted slowly across the globe over vast spans of geological time.
claims.source: Drifting-continents monograph (1915), dismissed by the geological establishment of the day

claims.claim_text: The landmasses continue to drift across the globe.
claims.source: Drifting-continents monograph (1915), dismissed by the geological establishment of the day

claims.claim_text: The continents were once joined together in a single primordial supercontinent.
claims.source: Drifting-continents monograph (1915), dismissed by the geological establishment of the day

claims.claim_text: The supercontinent broke apart in a distant epoch.
claims.source: Drifting-continents monograph (1915), dismissed by the geological establishment of the day

claims.claim_text: The fragments of the supercontinent have wandered to their present positions.
claims.source: Drifting-continents monograph (1915), dismissed by the geological establishment of the day

claims.claim_text: The correspondence of opposing coasts is a signature of ancient continental displacement.
claims.source: Drifting-continents monograph (1915), dismissed by the geological establishment of the day

claims.claim_text: The distribution of fossils across the oceans is a signature of ancient continental displacement.
claims.source: Drifting-continents monograph (1915), dismissed by the geological establishment of the day

claims.claim_text: The matching of geological formations across the oceans is a signature of ancient continental displacement.
claims.source: Drifting-continents monograph (1915), dismissed by the geological establishment of the day

claims.claim_text: The permanence of the continents and ocean basins is a settled principle of geology.
claims.source: Authoritative geology textbook, early-20th-century fixist consensus

claims.claim_text: The continents stand fixed upon the Earth's crust.
claims.source: Authoritative geology textbook, early-20th-century fixist consensus

claims.claim_text: The continents do not and cannot wander across the Earth's surface.
claims.source: Authoritative geology textbook, early-20th-century fixist consensus

claims.claim_text: No known force is remotely adequate to propel continental masses horizontally through the rigid rock beneath them.
claims.source: Authoritative geology textbook, early-20th-century fixist consensus

claims.claim_text: Where the same fossil species are found on lands now parted by ocean, the proper explanation is a former land bridge.
claims.source: Authoritative geology textbook, early-20th-century fixist consensus

claims.claim_text: Land bridges are isthmuses that have since foundered beneath the waves.
claims.source: Authoritative geology textbook, early-20th-century fixist consensus

claims.claim_text: The notion of drifting continents is a fanciful speculation.
claims.source: Authoritative geology textbook, early-20th-century fixist consensus

claims.claim_text: The notion of drifting continents is unsupported by any sound mechanism.
claims.source: Authoritative geology textbook, early-20th-century fixist consensus

claims.claim_text: The eastern seaboard of South America and the western seaboard of Africa mirror one another closely.
claims.source: Atlantic coastline correspondence survey

claims.claim_text: The outward bulge of Brazil corresponds to the inward curve of the Gulf of Guinea.
claims.source: Atlantic coastline correspondence survey

claims.claim_text: The geometric correspondence between the Atlantic coasts of South America and Africa is too exact across too great a length to be readily dismissed as coincidence.
claims.source: Atlantic coastline correspondence survey

claims.claim_text: The correspondence of the two coastlines suggests they were once joined and afterward drawn apart.
claims.source: Atlantic coastline correspondence survey

claims.claim_text: The seed-fern Glossopteris is found in the rocks of South America.
claims.source: Palaeontological fossil-distribution records

claims.claim_text: The seed-fern Glossopteris is found in the rocks of Africa.
claims.source: Palaeontological fossil-distribution records

claims.claim_text: The seed-fern Glossopteris is found in the rocks of India.
claims.source: Palaeontological fossil-distribution records

claims.claim_text: The seed-fern Glossopteris is found in the rocks of Australia.
claims.source: Palaeontological fossil-distribution records

claims.claim_text: Remains of the small freshwater reptile Mesosaurus occur on both sides of the South Atlantic.
claims.source: Palaeontological fossil-distribution records

claims.claim_text: Mesosaurus is characterized as a small freshwater reptile.
claims.source: Palaeontological fossil-distribution records

claims.claim_text: The presence of land-bound and freshwater species on landmasses now divided by broad oceans is difficult to explain unless those landmasses were formerly contiguous.
claims.source: Palaeontological fossil-distribution records

claims.claim_text: Geological structures broken off at one coastline resume at another coastline across the ocean.
claims.source: Cross-Atlantic rock-strata and glacial-deposit field notes

claims.claim_text: The mountain belts of the eastern coast of South America align with those of western Africa.
claims.source: Cross-Atlantic rock-strata and glacial-deposit field notes

claims.claim_text: The distinctive rock strata of the eastern coast of South America align with those of western Africa.
claims.source: Cross-Atlantic rock-strata and glacial-deposit field notes

claims.claim_text: The matching geological structures of South America and western Africa correspond in age, sequence, and character.
claims.source: Cross-Atlantic rock-strata and glacial-deposit field notes

claims.claim_text: Ancient glacial deposits of the same period are found across the southern continents.
claims.source: Cross-Atlantic rock-strata and glacial-deposit field notes

claims.claim_text: The positions of glacial deposits across the southern continents make little sense on the assumption of fixed continents.
claims.source: Cross-Atlantic rock-strata and glacial-deposit field notes

claims.claim_text: The glacial deposits across the southern continents fall into order if those lands were once assembled together nearer the pole.
claims.source: Cross-Atlantic rock-strata and glacial-deposit field notes

relationships.rationale: The claim that landmasses have drifted over geological time directly supports the idea that they are not fixed in place.
Ongoing drift of landmasses reinforces that they are not fixed in place.
A single primordial supercontinent that later broke apart implies the landmasses were never permanently fixed.
The breaking apart of the supercontinent is the mechanism by which the formerly joined continents separated.
The wandering of supercontinent fragments to present positions is a direct consequence of the supercontinent breaking apart.
The fragments wandering to their present positions is a concrete description of landmasses drifting over geological time.
Corresponding opposing coasts serve as evidence for ancient continental displacement, supporting the claim of past drift.
Fossil distribution across oceans is cited as evidence for ancient continental displacement and thus past drift.
Matching geological formations across oceans support the claim that landmasses have drifted over geological time.
Fixed continents are a direct expression of the permanence principle.
The impossibility of continental wandering directly upholds the principle of permanence of continents.
The absence of any known adequate force to move continents supports the claim that continents cannot wander.
Lack of a sound mechanism directly underpins the characterisation of continental drift as fanciful speculation.
No known adequate force is the core reason continental drift is said to be unsupported by any sound mechanism.
The land-bridge explanation directly contradicts the claim that contiguous landmasses (drift) are needed to explain shared species across oceans.
The land-bridge explanation contradicts treating fossil distribution as a signature of continental displacement.
Permanence of continents directly contradicts the claim that great landmasses are not fixed in place.
Continents standing fixed contradicts the claim that landmasses are not fixed in place.
The claim that continents cannot wander directly contradicts the claim that landmasses have drifted over geological time.
The claim that continents do not and cannot wander contradicts the claim that they continue to drift today.
Fixed continents cannot have wandered to present positions from a prior supercontinent.
Dismissing continental drift as fanciful speculation contradicts the foundational claim that landmasses are not fixed.
The close mirroring of South American and African seaboards is a concrete instance of the correspondence of opposing coasts cited as evidence of displacement.
The specific Brazil bulge/Gulf of Guinea correspondence is a detailed illustration of the general coastal mirroring claim.
The exactness of the geometric correspondence across a great length is the key evidence supporting the inference that the coasts were once joined.
The inference that the two coastlines were once joined supports the broader claim of a former single supercontinent.
Coastlines once joined and afterward drawn apart support the claim of a supercontinent that broke apart.
The close mirroring of the two seaboards is the basis for the claim of an exact geometric correspondence too great to be coincidence.
The specific bulge-to-curve correspondence reinforces the argument that the geometric match is too precise to be coincidental.
Finding Glossopteris in South America is part of the evidence that land-bound species appear on landmasses now divided by ocean.
Finding Glossopteris in Africa contributes to the pattern of a land-bound species spanning ocean-divided landmasses.
Finding Glossopteris in India adds another ocean-separated landmass to the pattern supporting former contiguity.
Finding Glossopteris in Australia further extends the pattern of a land-bound species across now-separated landmasses.
Mesosaurus occurring on both sides of the South Atlantic is a direct example of a species found on landmasses now divided by ocean.
Mesosaurus being a freshwater reptile makes its trans-oceanic distribution especially hard to explain without former contiguity, strengthening that claim.
The difficulty of explaining cross-ocean species distribution without former contiguity supports treating fossil distribution as a signature of ancient continental displacement.
Former contiguity of now-separated landmasses supports the claim of a single primordial supercontinent.
The claim that former contiguity best explains cross-ocean species contradicts the land-bridge explanation offered by the establishment.
Aligning mountain belts across the South Atlantic is a specific instance of geological structures resuming across an ocean.
Aligning rock strata across the South Atlantic is another specific instance of geological structures resuming across an ocean.
The correspondence in age, sequence, and character of the structures strengthens the claim that the mountain belts align across the ocean.
Matching age, sequence, and character also reinforces the alignment of rock strata across the ocean.
Geological structures broken off at one coast resuming at another is a concrete instance of matching geological formations across oceans.
Matching geological structures across oceans supports the inference of a former joined supercontinent.
The claim that glacial deposit positions make little sense under fixed continents contradicts the principle of continental permanence.
Glacial deposits falling into order when southern lands are assembled together near a pole supports the idea of a former supercontinent.
The coherence of glacial deposits under an assembled-continent model directly explains why their current positions make little sense with fixed continents.
The existence of same-period glacial deposits spread across the southern continents is the observation that is difficult to explain with fixed continents.
The same-period glacial deposits across southern continents are the data that fall into order when those lands are assembled near a pole.
